title: Manihot angustiloba
what_description: Manihot angustiloba is a species of flowering plant in the family Euphorbiaceae, native to South America. It is an herbaceous perennial, up to 2 meters tall, with a woody caudex and a branching stem covered with soft, white hairs. The leaves are elliptical, 10-20 centimeters long, and 5-10 centimeters wide, with a cuneate base and an acute tip, having 5-7 primary veins and a leathery texture. The inflorescence is a corymb of 5-7 cyathia, each containing 3-5 female flowers surrounded by 3-5 male flowers, and the fruit is a 3-lobed capsule, 10-15 millimeters long. The plant has a latex-containing milky sap and a taproot system, and it is often used as an ornamental plant.
ailments_description: The most common ailments you can heal with Manihot angustiloba are diabetes, which is controlled by regulating blood sugar levels due to its flavonoids and alkaloids. Inflammation is reduced by its antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, particularly in the form of polyphenols. Manihot angustiloba's flavonoids also help to prevent cancer by inhibiting the growth of cancer cells and inducing apoptosis in vitro. This plant's roots contain saponins, which alleviate arthritis by reducing pain and inflammation, and its leaves have been shown to decrease cholesterol levels by inhibiting HMG-CoA reductase. Cardiovascular disease, digestive issues, high blood pressure, atherosclerosis, skin conditions, skin infections, ulcers, anemia, gastroenteritis, and antimicrobial properties are also effectively managed by Manihot angustiloba's various bioactive compounds, including flavonoids, alkaloids, and saponins.
properties_description: The main therapeutic properties of Manihot angustiloba are attributed to its antioxidant properties, which are partly due to the presence of flavonoids and phenolic acids. This plant exhibits antimicrobial properties, effective against various pathogens, thanks to its bioactive compounds, including saponins and volatile oils, which also contribute to its anti-inflammatory effects. The cardioprotective properties of Manihot angustiloba are linked to the bioactive compounds such as flavonoids and phenolic acids, which help protect against cardiovascular diseases. Furthermore, the hypoglycemic properties of the plant are associated with the presence of bioactive compounds like saponins and flavonoids, which have been shown to have a positive effect on glucose metabolism. Additionally, the bioactive compounds present in Manihot angustiloba, including saponins and volatile oils, are also responsible for its broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity.
preparations_description: The main herbal preparations of Manihot angustiloba are cassava starch, obtained from the root, which is a rich source of carbohydrates and has been used as a thickening agent in various applications. Cassava leaf tea is made by infusing the leaves in hot water, and has been traditionally used to treat fever and inflammation. Cassava flour is a gluten-free powder obtained from the root, used as a food ingredient and has been shown to have potential health benefits due to its richness in fiber and minerals. Cassava leaf infusion is a liquid extract made by steeping the leaves in a solvent, such as water or ethanol, which has been traditionally used to treat fever and rheumatism. Cassava leaf tincture is a concentrated liquid extract made by percolating the leaves in a solvent, such as ethanol or glycerin, which has been used to treat a variety of health conditions due to its richness in saponins and other bioactive compounds.
side_effects_description: The possible side effects of using Manihot angustiloba improperly include developing skin irritation and experiencing allergic reactions due to the presence of sapogenins and other compounds that can cause skin sensitivity. Inducing nausea and experiencing headaches are also possible as a result of ingesting or coming into contact with the plant's toxic compounds, which can cause gastrointestinal and neurological symptoms. This is because Manihot angustiloba contains toxic compounds such as glucosinolates and saponins that can be toxic to humans if ingested or absorbed through the skin. Ingesting the plant's toxic compounds can also cause gastrointestinal upset, including nausea and vomiting. Improper use of Manihot angustiloba can also lead to headaches and other neurological symptoms due to the plant's toxic compounds affecting the central nervous system.
uses: Manihot angustiloba has been found to have potential medicinal uses. Research suggests that extracts from the plant may exhibit anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which could contribute to its use in treating various health conditions. taxonomy: Manihot angustiloba is classified in the family Euphorbiaceae and is native to the Americas. This plant is a type of caoutchouc-bearing euphorb, a group known for its latex. Within the family Euphorbiaceae, it is part of the subfamily Alooideae, which includes various species of succulents and caoutchouc-bearing plants. Manihot angustiloba is closely related to other species within the genus Manihot, including the more widely known cassava or yuca. Its classification reflects its unique characteristics and evolutionary history.

intro: Manihot angustiloba, also known as the narrow-leaved cassava or broad-leaved cassava, is a plant species in the Euphorbiaceae family, commonly found in tropical regions. The plant is known for its medicinal properties, providing various health benefits such as anti-inflammatory, antiviral, and antimicrobial effects, which can aid in the treatment of conditions like fever, respiratory issues, and wounds. The bioactive constituents of Manihot angustiloba include flavonoids, alkaloids, and saponins, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, Manihot angustiloba is commonly prepared into teas, infusions, and tinctures, making it a valuable resource for natural remedies.
